content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

August 29, 1979

Hank Doerfling
California Coastal Commission
631 Howard Street, Fourth Floor
San Francisco, CA 94105

Re: Opinion Request
    No. 79-003

Dear Mr. Doerfling:

As you may recall from our letter of February 21, 1979, a response to your request for a formal opinion regarding the Political Reform Act's disqualification provision would be delayed until additional information was received. That additional information has now been provided herein. Because your request raises no substantial questions involving an interpretation of the Political Reform Act, no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following informal advice will be helpful.

The facts, as I understand them, are as follows: You serve as a board member of the South Coast Regional Coastal Commission and the California Coastal Commission. In addition, you are employed as a staff engineer for Hughes Aircraft Company, which is wholly owned by the Hughes Medical Institute, a charitable organization controlled by its board of directors. The Howard Hughes estate has no interest in the Medical Institute. Hughes Aircraft was formerly owned by Howard Hughes. However, Hughes donated his interest in Hughes Aircraft to the Institute. The aircraft company currently rents space for its main headquarters from the Summa Corporation. Summa was wholly owned by Howard Hughes and is currently controlled by the administrators of his estate pending the outcome of probate litigation.

Summa Corporation owns approximately 350 acres of undeveloped land within the jurisdiction of the South Coast Regional Coastal Commission. Some of the facilities Hughes Aircraft leases from Summa are adjacent to Summa's undeveloped

Hank Doerfling
August 29, 1979
Page Two


land. It is anticipated that the Summa Corporation will
seek Coastal Commission approval to develop its land. You
have asked whether the Political Reform Act requires you to
disqualify yourself from participating in those Commission
decisions concerning Summa's property. In addition, you
have asked whether it is permissible for you to participate
in other Coastal Commission decisions that may materially
affect the Summa Corporation.

Government Code Section 87100 provides,

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.

Government Code Section 87103 states,

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

     (a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000);

                    . . .

     (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250)
or more in value received by or promised to the public
official within twelve months prior to the time when
the decision is made;....

The Political Reform Act requires you to refrain from
participating in those decisions that may materially affect
your sources of income or business entities in which you
have investments. The facts provided lead us to conclude
that you do not have an investment in or receive income from
the Summa Corporation.

Hank Doerfling
August 29, 1979
Page Three


     The fact that Hughes Aircraft and Summa Corporation
were at one time both controlled by Howard Hughes raises a
question as to whether Summa Corporation should be considered
a source of income to you. If the two entities are owned or
controlled by the same person or group of persons, your
receipt of income from Hughes Aircraft could be deemed as
also coming from Summa Corporation. See Sections 82005,
82047. However, annual Franchise Tax reports of Hughes
Aircraft, the Medical Institute and Summa submitted to authori-
ties in Delaware, the state in which these entities are
incorporated, indicate that Summa, on the one hand, and
Hughes Aircraft and the Medical Institute, on the other, are
owned and controlled by different entities and persons. No
officer or director of Summa is a director of officer of
Hughes Aircraft or the Medical Institute, and no officer or
director of Hughes Aircraft or the Medical Institute is an
officer or director of Summa. Although Howard Hughes may
have had the ability to control all three entities, his
death has resulted in a division of ownership and control
whereby Summa is controlled by one set of individuals and
the Institute and Hughes Aircraft by another set. Under
these circumstances, we conclude that Summa Corporation is
an entity separate from Hughes Aircraft and the Medical
Institute and is not a source of income to you.

     As you may be aware, litigation is currently pending
between the persons who control the Summa Corporation and
those who control the Medical Institute and Hughes Aircraft.
The outcome of that litigation could result in changes in
the control of both entities. If that occurs, it may be
necessary to re-evaluate our conclusion that Summa Corpora-
tion is not a source of income to you.

     Because you do not have investments or receive income
from Summa Corporation, you may participate in those Coastal
Commission decisions that materially affect the Summa Corpora-
tion. However, if it is reasonable foreseeable that any of
the decisions concerning Summa could also materially affect
your employer, Hughes Aircraft, you must disqualify yourself.
For example, it is possible that Commission decisions concerning
Summa's development proposals might have a material financial
impact upon businesses, including Hughes Aircraft, which are
located adjacent to the Summa property. See Opinion requested
by Gary G. Gillmore, 3 FPPC Opinions 38 (No. 76-089, April 6,
1977). Whether disqualification would be required turns
upon the specific development proposal and its possible
affects on surrounding businesses.

Hank Doerfling
August 29, 1979
Page Four

I hope that our advice is helpful, if we can be of
further assistance please contact Dwight Dickerson, an attorney
in our Conflicts of Interest Division.

If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request,
you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

DED:kp

cc: Natalie E. West

State of California, Edmund G. Brown Jr., Governor

Request No. 79 003

California Coastal Commission
631 Howard Street, 4th floor
San Francisco, California 94105
(415) 543-8555

Date Received 8-7-79

Response Due 2-21-49


January 31, 1979


Ted Prim
Chairman, Conflicts of Interest Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Prim:

     This letter requests a formal opinion of the Fair Political Practices Commission ("FPPC") pursuant to Government Code (Section 83114). I am a member of the South Coast Regional Coastal Commission and of the California Coastal Commission. I am employed as a staff engineer in computer systems and software, Space and Communications Group of Hughes Aircraft Company ("Hughes Aircraft"). Hughes Aircraft is wholly owned by Howard Hughes Medical Institute. It is my understanding that Hughes Airwest is owned by the Summa Corporation ("Summa") and that Hughes Helicopters is a division of Summa. Despite the similarity in names, I am not aware of any business relationships between Hughes Aircraft and any of the companies owned or controlled by Summa.

     Summa owns approximately 350 acres of undeveloped land in Los Angeles County, which it wishes to develop. Hughes Aircraft's corporate headquarters and airstrip are located adjacent to the undeveloped site. Hughes Aircraft leases these facilities from Summa but intends to build its own headquarters at another site by 1981. I would like the opinion of the FPPC as to whether I am required to disqualify myself from participation in decisions that may impact on this area, including the following:

     1. Development of an issue identification and work program for Marina del Rey and other areas in Los Angeles County. Issue identification is intended to identify existing or potential conflicts between the policies of the Coastal Act and (1) existing conditions in the coastal zone, as well as (2) development proposed by existing policies or development plans of Los Angeles County. 14 Cal. Adm. Code, Section 13503 (a).

State of California, Edmund G. Brown Jr., Governor

California Coastal Commission
631 Howard Street, 4th floor
San Francisco, California 94105
(415) 543-8555


Mr. Ted Prim
January 31, 1979
Page 2

2. Review of local coastal programs, consisting of land use plans and
comprehensive zoning ordinances proposed by Los Angeles County and other
local governments for that portion of the coastal zone located in the South
Coast Region. Public Resources Code, Section 30500, 30511.

3. Processing of applications for coastal development permits for any de-
velopments proposed in the coastal zone. Public Resources Code, Section
30600 et seq.

All of these actions are initially carried out by the Regional Commission
and may be reviewed by the State Coastal Commission. Since I am a member of
both agencies, I will be involved in both the formulation and review of the
planning and permit processes. Although some of the decisions described above
may have a material financial effect on Summa, it is unclear to me what, if any,
impact they could have on Hughes Aircraft. However, several questions have been
raised with respect to my continued participation in decisions that may affect
Summa.Consequently, a formal opinion of the FPPC is requested.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
HANK DOERFLING

jh

[Logo of California Coastal Commission at bottom]

cc: 
Natalie E. West
sections.conclusion: Because you do not have investments or receive income from Summa Corporation, you may participate in those Coastal Commission decisions that materially affect the Summa Corporation. However, if it is reasonably foreseeable that any of the decisions concerning Summa could also materially affect your employer, Hughes Aircraft, you must disqualify yourself. For example, it is possible that Commission decisions concerning Summa's development proposals might have a material financial impact upon businesses, including Hughes Aircraft, which are located adjacent to the Summa property. Whether disqualification would be required turns upon the specific development proposal and its possible effects on surrounding businesses.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a California Coastal Commission member who is employed by Hughes Aircraft must disqualify himself from participating in Coastal Commission decisions affecting Summa Corporation, which owns land adjacent to Hughes Aircraft facilities and was formerly controlled by the same person (Howard Hughes) who controlled Hughes Aircraft.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The requestor need not disqualify himself from decisions affecting Summa Corporation since he has no financial interest in it, but must disqualify himself if any Summa decisions could materially affect Hughes Aircraft, his employer, which requires case-by-case analysis of specific development proposals.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the original incoming request letter (dated January 31, 1979) and the FPPC response letter (dated August 29, 1979). Response indicates that while no formal opinion would be issued, informal advice is provided. The document is an advice letter responding to a formal opinion request under Government Code Section 83114.

CONCLUSION: Because you do not have investments or receive income from Summa Corporation, you may participate in those Coastal Commission decisions that materially affect the Summa Corporation. However, if it is reasonably foreseeable that any of the decisions concerning Summa could also materially affect your employer, Hughes Aircraft, you must disqualify yourself. For example, it is possible that Commission decisions concerning Summa's development proposals might have a material financial impact upon businesses, including Hughes Aircraft, which are located adjacent to the Summa property. Whether disqualification would be required turns upon the specific development proposal and its possible effects on surrounding businesses.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises a California Coastal Commission member and Hughes Aircraft employee that he need not disqualify himself from decisions affecting Summa Corporation (which owns adjacent undeveloped land), since Summa is separately owned and controlled from Hughes Aircraft despite their historical common ownership by Howard Hughes. However, disqualification is required if specific Summa development decisions could materially affect Hughes Aircraft.
